1: Wᵉʾēlleh bᵉnê hamᵉdînāh — 'Now these are the people of the province' (bᵉnê hamᵉdînāh = 'sons/children of the province'). Mᵉdînāh is the Aramaic/Persian term for the administrative district of Yehud (Judah) within the satrapy of Abar-Nahara (Beyond the River). The same list appears in Neh 7:6-72 with many variant numbers, indicating the two texts use a common source (perhaps an official Persian administrative register) that was transmitted differently in each manuscript tradition. The introduction identifies the returnees as those Nebuchadnezzar had carried to Babylon (šᵉlāḥam nᵉḇûḵaḏneṣṣar melek bāḇel lᵉḇāḇel wayyāšûḇû lîrûšālaim wîhûdāh) — the return framed as exact reversal: same cities, same people, going back.
2: The eleven leaders who returned with Zerubbabel: Zᵉrubbāḇel (= Babylonian Zēr-Bābili = 'seed of Babylon' or 'offspring of Babylon') is the Davidic prince, grandson of Jehoiachin (1 Chr 3:19; cf. Matt 1:12). Yēšûaʿ (= 'YHWH saves' — the same name as Joshua/Jesus) is the high priest, son of Jozadak (the priest taken captive to Babylon, 1 Chr 6:15). These two — Zerubbabel (royal) and Jeshua (priestly) — are the twin leaders of the restoration, foreshadowing the priestly-royal partnership in Zechariah's visions (Zech 4:14 'two anointed ones'). Nehemiah here is not the later governor (who came much later); Seraiah, Mordecai, and others in the list appear in different forms between Ezra and Neh 7 (Neh 7:7 has 'Nahamani' instead of 'Nehemiah' in some readings). The total number of returned laypeople (mispar ʾanšê ʿam yiśrāʾēl) follows in the subsequent verses.
3: Bᵉnê pāròš — descendants of Parosh, 2,172. Ezra 2 = 2,172; Neh 7:8 = 2,172 (agreement). Pāròš = 'flea' — an ironic family name (such names appear throughout the census). This is the largest single family in the lay list. The term bᵉnê (sons of) is the standard marker for the family-based (patrilineal) sections (vv3-20), as opposed to ʾanšê (men of, vv21-35) for the geography-based sections. The family-based section names ancestors by eponymous head; the totals represent households attached to that ancestral name.
4: Bᵉnê šᵉpaṭyāh — descendants of Shephatiah, 372. Ezra = 372; Neh 7:9 = 372 (agreement). Šᵉpaṭyāh = 'YHWH has judged' — a theophoric name. The family appears in Neh 11:4 as part of the Jerusalem settlement list. The numbers in this verse and surrounding ones show that family sizes in the returnee list range from the very large (Parosh at 2,172) to the quite small (e.g., Ater at 98 in v16), reflecting the varied fortunes of Judean families in exile.
5: Bᵉnê āraḥ — descendants of Arah, 775. Here a significant Ezra/Neh variant: Neh 7:10 reads 652 (not 775). The Arah discrepancy of 123 persons is one of the largest numerical differences between the parallel lists. No fully satisfactory explanation exists: suggested causes include scribal error in one tradition, intentional revision (perhaps reflecting a later updated count), or use of different counting methods (some may count heads of household while others count all family members). A descendant of Arah returns in Ezra 2:5 context as a family leader; in Neh 6:18 a daughter of Meshullam son of Berechiah is described as wife of a Tobiah, whose father-in-law is Shecaniah son of Arah — suggesting the family maintained prominence in the community.
6: Bᵉnê paḥaṯ-môʾāḇ — descendants of Pahath-moab ('governor of Moab'), 2,812, through the clans of Jeshua and Joab. Neh 7:11 = 2,818 (difference of 6). Pahath-moab is an unusual family name suggesting an ancestor who held the office of governor over Moab, or who was associated with Moabite territory. This family is subdivided by clans (mišpaḥôt): Jeshua and Joab, indicating a large multi-clan family group. The family name reappears in Ezra 8:4 (among those who return with Ezra) and in Neh 3:11; 10:14 (in later community contexts), suggesting a prominent multi-generational family.
7: Bᵉnê ʿēlām — descendants of Elam, 1,254. Neh 7:12 = 1,254 (agreement). Elam is a common personal name (cf. multiple Elamites in 1 Chr genealogies); this is the 'first Elam' (there is a 'second' or 'other Elam' in v31, also 1,254 — an unusual coincidence of identical numbers for different families). The name may reflect a Judahite ancestor associated with the region of Elam (modern SW Iran) or simply a common Semitic name.
8: Bᵉnê zaṯṯû — descendants of Zattu, 945. Neh 7:13 = 845 (difference of 100). The Ezra/Neh difference here is exactly 100, which often indicates a place where a century was either accidentally added or omitted in transmission. Zattu appears in Neh 10:14 as a signatory of the covenant renewal under Nehemiah — the family head participates in the formal covenant reinstatement generations later.
9: Bᵉnê zakkay — descendants of Zaccai, 760. Neh 7:14 = 760 (agreement). Zakkay is related to the root zāḵāh (to be clean, pure, acquitted) — a name meaning 'pure' or 'innocent.' The precise agreement between Ezra and Neh here, against many verses that differ, suggests this particular entry was transmitted carefully or that the number was memorable (760 is a round-ish number but not obviously round).
10: Bᵉnê bānî — descendants of Bani, 642. Neh 7:15 reads 'Binnui' with 648 (both name and number differ). The Ezra/Neh variant bānî vs binnûy may reflect confusion between similar-looking names in the ancestral register. Bani is a very common name in post-exilic texts (appears in Ezra 10:29, 34, 38; Neh 3:17; 8:7; 10:13-14 etc.) — a frequent occurrence that makes distinguishing which Bani appears in which context an ongoing challenge.
11: Bᵉnê bēḇay — descendants of Bebai, 623. Neh 7:16 = 628 (difference of 5). The name Bēḇay is likely a hypocoristic (shortened pet name) from a theophoric name; its exact etymology is unclear. The small Ezra/Neh difference of 5 may be a simple scribal transposition (3 → 8) in one transmission line.
12: Bᵉnê ʿazgaḏ — descendants of Azgad, 1,222. Neh 7:17 = 2,322 (a very large difference of 1,100!). The discrepancy for Azgad is the largest in the list — over 1,000 persons different. Azgad appears in Ezra 8:12 with 110 returning with Ezra on the second return (a smaller subset), and in Neh 10:15. The extreme Ezra/Neh difference may reflect the family's actual growth between the first return (Ezra 2) and a later updated register, or a major scribal error in one tradition.
13: Bᵉnê ʾăḏônîqām — descendants of Adonikam ('my lord has arisen'), 666. Neh 7:18 = 667 (difference of 1). The name Adonikam is theophoric with ʾādôn (lord/master). The number 666 draws later attention in Christian interpretation given Rev 13:18, but in context it is simply a census figure; the number appears without special significance in the ancient Near Eastern administrative record.
14: Bᵉnê ḇigway — descendants of Bigvai, 2,056. Neh 7:19 = 2,067 (difference of 11). Bigvai is an Iranian name (cf. Bagoi = 'given by God/Baga') — indicating a family head who bore a Persian name, perhaps acquired during the exile. A Bigvai appears in Ezra 8:14 with 70 (plus 68) more returning with Ezra. The name appears also in the list of Zerubbabel's leading companions (v2).
15: Bᵉnê ʿāḏîn — descendants of Adin ('voluptuous, delicate'), 454. Neh 7:20 = 655 (a difference of 201 — very large). The name ʿāḏîn derives from ʿāḏan (to be delicate, voluptuous). A significant Ezra/Neh discrepancy again; like Azgad, the difference suggests either a major scribal error or the use of different administrative sources. Adin appears in Ezra 8:6 (50 more returning with Ezra) and Neh 10:16.
16: Bᵉnê āṭēr lᵉḥizqiyyāh — descendants of Ater through Hezekiah, 98. Neh 7:21 = 98 (agreement). The unusual phrase 'Ater through Hezekiah' (lᵉḥizqiyyāh) qualifies the family by its most famous member — Hezekiah, though likely not the king. The family is identified by this prominent ancestor within the Ater line. With only 98, this is one of the smallest lay families.
17: Bᵉnê ḇēṣay — descendants of Bezai, 323. Neh 7:23 = 324 (difference of 1). A minor variant; the name Bezai is otherwise rare. The small difference (1 person) between Ezra and Neh for this family may reflect a later addition to the register or a single counting difference.
18: Bᵉnê yôrāh — descendants of Jorah, 112. Neh 7:24 reads 'Hariph' with 112 (name differs, number agrees). The Ezra/Neh name variant (Jorah vs Hariph) with identical numbers is unusual — the same number assigned to two different names suggests either the ancestral name changed between traditions or one was a sobriquet of the other. Yôrāh = 'early rain' or possibly from yārāh (to throw/shoot); ḥārîp = 'autumn/sharp' — semantically unrelated names.
19: Bᵉnê ḥāšum — descendants of Hashum, 223. Neh 7:22 = 328 (difference of 105). Another significant Ezra/Neh discrepancy. Hashum appears in Neh 8:4 (standing with Ezra at the Torah reading), 10:18. The family is active in community leadership across Ezra-Nehemiah contexts.
20: Bᵉnê gibbār — descendants of Gibbar, 95. Neh 7:25 reads 'Gibeon' with 95 (name differs; number agrees). The Ezra gibbār ('mighty man') vs Neh 7 giḇʿôn ('Gibeon') with identical numbers strongly suggests a textual confusion between the personal name Gibbar and the place name Gibeon — they look similar in the Hebrew script. The Neh 7 tradition likely preserves the geographic reading (placing this entry in the transition toward the place-based section of v21+), while Ezra preserves a personal-name reading.
21: Bᵉnê bêt-leḥem — men of Bethlehem, 123. With this verse the list transitions from bᵉnê (descendants = family-based) to ʾanšê (men of = place-based). Both Bethlehem and Netophah (v22) are villages near each other in the Judean highlands south of Jerusalem. Bethlehem as David's city carries dynastic significance; its returnees include those associated with the Davidic heartland. Neh 7:26 groups Bethlehem and Netophah together for a combined total of 188, whereas Ezra separates them (123 + 56 = 179 — a difference of 9).
22: Anšê nᵉṭōpāh — men of Netophah, 56. Netophah appears in Jer 40:8 as the hometown of military captains who joined Gedaliah after the fall of Jerusalem. The Netophathites are mentioned in 2 Sam 23:28-29 as the hometown of two of David's mighty men — a village with Davidic military associations. Its returnees number only 56, a small village contingent.
23: Anšê ʿănātôt — men of Anathoth, 128. Anathoth is the Levitical city in Benjamin where Jeremiah was from (Jer 1:1) — the prophet whose 70-year oracle (cited in Ezra 1:1) shaped the theology of the whole return. The returnees from Anathoth thus come from the very city associated with the prophet whose word the return fulfills. Neh 7:27 = 128 (agreement).
24: Bᵉnê azmāwet — men/descendants of Azmaveth, 42. Neh 7:28 reads 'Beth-azmaveth' with 42 (number agrees; Neh adds 'Beth-' prefix indicating the village). Azmaveth is a village north of Jerusalem; the 'Beth-' in Neh clarifies it is the settlement rather than a personal name. The small number (42) indicates a tiny hamlet's returnees.
25: Bᵉnê qiryat-ʿārîm kᵉpîrāh ûḇᵉʾērôt — men of Kiriath-arim, Chephirah, and Beeroth, 743. These are three Benjaminite cities grouped together (they appear as a Gibeonite-allied cluster in Josh 9:17). Kiriath-arim/Kiriath-jearim is where the ark of the covenant rested before David brought it to Jerusalem (1 Sam 7:1-2; 2 Sam 6:3). Grouping three towns for a single total is unusual in the list and may reflect that they shared a common administrative register or were treated as a single administrative unit. Neh 7:29 = 743 (agreement).
26: Bᵉnê hārāmāh wāgeḇaʿ — men of Ramah and Geba, 621. Neh 7:30 = 621 (agreement). Ramah and Geba are Benjaminite border towns north of Jerusalem (cf. Isa 10:29 for the Assyrian advance through these settlements). Grouped as a pair, the two towns are treated as a single administrative unit with 621 returnees.
27: Anšê miḵmās — men of Michmas, 122. Neh 7:31 = 122 (agreement). Michmas (Michmash) is the site of Jonathan's famous single-handed exploit against the Philistines (1 Sam 13-14). Its inclusion in the returnee list confirms the restoration of settlement across the Benjaminite highlands that the Babylonian deportation had depopulated. The number 122 reflects a moderate-sized settlement.
28: Anšê bêt-ʾēl wᵉhāʿay — men of Bethel and Ai, 223. Neh 7:32 = 123 (a difference of 100 — exactly one century, suggesting scribal addition/omission). Bethel is the site of Jacob's vision (Gen 28) and later one of Jeroboam's golden-calf shrines (1 Kgs 12:29). Its inclusion without comment in the returnee list is notable — the city with northern kingdom idolatrous associations is resettled by returning Judahites. Ai (et-Tell) is the site of Joshua's conquest (Josh 7-8) — another conquest-era landmark being repopulated.
29: Bᵉnê nᵉḇô — descendants of Nebo, 52. Neh 7:33 = 52 (agreement). Nebo is a Moabite/Transjordanian place name (Num 32:3; the mountain from which Moses viewed Canaan); here it designates a Judean village west of the Jordan, possibly a secondary settlement named after the original Transjordanian site. The small number (52) indicates a minor community.
30: Bᵉnê magbîš — descendants of Magbish, 156. This entry has no parallel in Neh 7, which is one of several significant differences between the two lists. Magbish is otherwise unattested as a place or person name in the OT, making this a hapax toponym. Its absence from Neh 7 raises the question of whether Neh 7's list is later or earlier — if the community of Magbish had dissolved or been absorbed by Nehemiah's time, it might not appear in the later register.
31: Bᵉnê ʿēlām ʾaḥēr — descendants of the other Elam, 1,254. The exact same number (1,254) as the 'first' Elam in v7 is remarkable — mathematical coincidence seems unlikely, and the repetition may reflect a scribal duplication, a textual problem, or the merging of two branches of the same family using the same total. Neh 7:34 = 1,254 (agreement). The qualifier ʾaḥēr (other/another) explicitly distinguishes this from v7's Elam.
32: Bᵉnê ḥārîm — descendants of Harim, 320. Neh 7:35 = 320 (agreement). Harim (ḥārîm = 'snub-nosed' or 'devoted') is a priestly name in the list of 24 priestly divisions (1 Chr 24:8, the third division); this may be a lay family named after a priestly ancestor, or a separate lay family with the same name. A priestly Harim family appears in v39.
33: Bᵉnê lōḏ ḥāḏîḏ wᵉʾônô — men of Lod, Hadid, and Ono, 725. Neh 7:37 = 721 (difference of 4). These three towns are in the Shephelah/coastal plain northwest of Jerusalem, near what is now the Tel Aviv area. Lod (Lydda) appears in Acts 9:32-38 as the site of Peter's healing ministry. The grouping of three towns with a combined total is the same pattern as Kiriath-arim/Chephirah/Beeroth (v25) — administrative clustering of associated settlements.
34: Bᵉnê yᵉrîḥô — men of Jericho, 345. Neh 7:36 = 345 (agreement). Jericho, the 'City of Palms,' is the first city conquered in Joshua's campaign (Josh 6) — its repopulation in the returnee list signals the restoration of the Jordan Valley settlement. The Judean presence at Jericho would have been in the oasis zone east of Jerusalem; its returnees are part of the broader reoccupation of the historical Israelite territory.
35: Bᵉnê sᵉnāʾāh — descendants of Senaah, 3,630. Neh 7:38 = 3,930 (difference of 300). With 3,630 returnees, Senaah is by far the largest group in the lay list — larger than even Parosh (2,172). The name Senaah is otherwise unknown as a person or place name; some scholars suggest it may be a variant of Magdalsenna or simply an unidentified village. The very large number suggests either a major settlement or a group designation that includes people from multiple origins.
36: Hakkōhănîm bᵉnê yᵉdaʿyāh lᵉḇêt yēšûaʿ — the priests: the descendants of Jedaiah of the house of Jeshua, 973. With v36 the list transitions to the cultic personnel (priests, vv36-39; Levites, v40; singers, v41; gatekeepers, v42). Jedaiah is a priestly family name (appears in the 24 priestly divisions, 1 Chr 24:7); the qualifier 'of the house of Jeshua' connects them to the high-priestly family of Jeshua son of Jozadak (the high priest of the restoration). This is the most prominent priestly family — associated with the high priest himself. Neh 7:39 = 973 (agreement).
37: Bᵉnê ʾimmēr — descendants of Immer, 1,052. Neh 7:40 = 1,052 (agreement). Immer is the priestly division that appears in 1 Chr 24:14 (the sixteenth division); the family is associated with the temple priesthood. In Jer 20:1 the chief officer (pāqîḏ nāgîḏ) of the temple is Pashhur son of Immer — suggesting the family held administrative authority in the pre-exilic temple. The 1,052 returning Immer priests constitute the second-largest priestly family in the list.
38: Bᵉnê pašḥûr — descendants of Pashhur, 1,247. Neh 7:41 = 1,247 (agreement). Pashhur is the largest priestly family in the returnee list (1,247). The name Pashhur is associated with multiple temple officials in pre-exilic texts (Jer 20:1; 21:1; 38:1). The family appears in Ezra 10:22 as among those who had married foreign women — maintaining its prominence into the later post-exilic period.
39: Bᵉnê ḥārîm — descendants of Harim, 1,017. Neh 7:42 = 1,017 (agreement). A priestly Harim distinct from the lay Harim of v32. Harim appears in the priestly rotation divisions (1 Chr 24:8). Four priestly families return in total (vv36-39): Jedaiah/Jeshua (973), Immer (1,052), Pashhur (1,247), and Harim (1,017) — a total of approximately 4,289 returning priests. The Levites (v40) by contrast number only 74 — a disproportionately small Levitical contingent that creates a clergy-imbalance in the early restoration community.
40: HalᵉwiyyiM bᵉnê yēšûaʿ wᵉqadmîʾēl lᵉḇᵉnê hôḏawyāh šiḇʿîm wᵉʾarbaʿāh — the Levites: descendants of Jeshua and Kadmiel through Hodaviah, 74. Neh 7:43 = 74 (agreement). The striking fact is the disproportion: 4,289 priests but only 74 Levites returned. The Levitical shortage in the early restoration explains why Ezra 8:15-20 records Ezra's deliberate effort to recruit Levites for the second return (he found none and had to persuade specific individuals). The causes of Levitical reluctance to return are not stated; scholars suggest the Levites had lower status and fewer economic incentives in Babylon, or that Levitical families were more integrated into Babylonian life. The three Levitical families (Jeshua, Kadmiel, Hodaviah) may be sub-clans of the broader Levitical tribe.
41: Hamᵉšōrᵉrîm bᵉnê ʾāsāp mēʾāh ûšmōnāh wᵉʿešrîm — the singers: descendants of Asaph, 128. Neh 7:44 = 148 (difference of 20). The singers (mᵉšōrᵉrîm) are listed separately from the Levites — in Chronicles they are a sub-group of Levites, but Ezra treats them as a distinct category. Asaph was David's chief musician (1 Chr 16:5; 2 Chr 5:12) and the head of one of the three Levitical singing guilds (Asaph, Heman, Jeduthun/Ethan). The 128 (or 148) returnees represent the Asaphite singing guild — the only one of the three guilds to return with the first wave, reflecting Asaph's prominence in the cult music tradition preserved through the exile.
42: Bᵉnê haššōʿărîm — the gatekeepers: six families (Shallum, Ater, Talmon, Akkub, Hatita, Shobai), 139 total. Neh 7:45 = 138 (difference of 1). Gatekeepers (šōʿărîm) formed a distinct Levitical sub-group responsible for security and access control at the temple gates. The six families listed here correspond to gatekeeping clans documented in 1 Chr 26:1-19. In the post-exilic period the gatekeepers remained a distinct guilded group; Nehemiah 11:19 notes 172 gatekeepers in Jerusalem, showing their growth from the 139 who returned. The role of gatekeeper (šōʿēr) is etymologically related to the gate (šaʿar) — threshold guardians of the sanctuary.
43: Hannᵉtînîm — the Nethinim (temple servants) begin here. Nᵉtînîm derives from nātan (to give) in the passive/Niphal sense = 'given/dedicated ones' — persons given or dedicated to temple service. The tradition in Num 31:40-47 traces temple-servant origins to the Midianite women and children given to the Levites after the Midian war; Ezra 8:20 says David and his officials had designated them for the Levites. Many Nethinim names in vv43-54 are foreign (non-Hebrew), suggesting their origins as captive/foreign servants integrated into the temple service over generations: Ziha (Egyptian?), Hasupha, Tabbaoth ('seal-ring' rings = Semitic).
44: Bᵉnê qērōs sîʿāh wᵉpāḏôn — Keros, Siaha, and Padon. Qērōs may be related to a root meaning 'bent/curved'; Sîʿāh is possibly related to 'mud/slime'; Pāḏôn = 'redemption' (Hebrew root pdh) — a name suggesting a servant who was redeemed from slavery into temple service. The variety of etymological backgrounds in the Nethinim names reflects the diverse origins of this group over the centuries they served the temple.
45: Bᵉnê lᵉḇānāh ḥăgāḇāh wᵉʾakkûḇ — Lebanah, Hagabah, and Akkub. Lᵉḇānāh = 'white/moon' (possibly connected to Lebanon); Ḥăgāḇāh = 'locust' (related to ḥāgāḇ, the locust species); Akkûḇ = possibly from Akkadian personal name. These are clearly names from diverse linguistic backgrounds, confirming the Nethinim's mixed heritage.
46: Bᵉnê ḥăgāḇ šalmay wᵉḥānān — Hagab, Shalmai, and Hanan. Ḥăgāḇ = 'locust' (same root as Hagabah v45); Šalmay may be Aramaic or Persian; Ḥānān = 'gracious' (Hebrew) — showing that Hebrew theophoric names also appear among the Nethinim, those whose families had been integrated into the Israelite community over generations.
47: Bᵉnê giddēl gaḥar wᵉrᵉʾāyāh — Giddel, Gahar, and Reaiah. Giddēl = 'greatly magnified' (Hebrew root gāḏal); Gaḥar may be foreign; Rᵉʾāyāh = 'YHWH has seen' (theophoric). The mix of names — Hebrew theophoric, Hebrew descriptive, and possibly foreign — in a single Nethinim group verse illustrates the ongoing process of naming-integration as families served in the temple community across generations.
48: Bᵉnê rᵉṣîn nᵉqôḏāʾ wᵉgazzām — Rezin, Nekoda, and Gazzam. Rᵉṣîn is the name of the king of Damascus in the Syro-Ephraimite War (Isa 7:1) — whether this family took its name from an Aramean ancestor or the name is coincidental is unknown. Nᵉqôḏāʾ = 'spotted/distinctive' (related to nāqōd = speckled); Gazzām = 'locust, caterpillar' (one of several insect names in the Nethinim list — see Hagab v46, Hagabah v45).
49: Bᵉnê ʿuzzāʾ pāsēaḥ ûḇēsay — Uzza, Paseah, and Besai. ʿUzzāʾ = 'strength' (same name as the man who died touching the ark, 2 Sam 6:7); Pāsēaḥ = 'limping' (possibly from a lame ancestor; cf. Mephibosheth's lameness); Bēsay = uncertain etymology. Paseah appears in Neh 3:6 as a repairman of the Old Gate — showing a Nethinim descendant taking an active role in the later community.
50: Bᵉnê ʾasnāh mᵉʿûnîm nᵉpîsîm — Asnah, Meunim, and Nephisim. Mᵉʿûnîm is the name of the Meunites, a group from the Ma'an region near Petra (cf. 2 Chr 26:7) — a Nethinim family of south Arabian origin. Nᵉpîsîm may relate to Naphish, son of Ishmael (Gen 25:15; 1 Chr 1:31; cf. 1 Chr 5:19 where the Reubenites war against Hagrites including Nephish). These names point to the absorption of various conquered or subject peoples into the temple service system over the pre-exilic period.
51: Bᵉnê baqbûq ḥăqûpāʾ wᵉḥarḥûr — Bakbuk, Hakupha, and Harhur. Baqbûq = 'bottle/flask' (onomatopoeic for the gurgling sound of an emptying bottle, cf. baqbuq in modern Hebrew); Ḥăqûpāʾ and Ḥarḥûr are names of uncertain etymology, possibly foreign. The sound-symbolism of some Nethinim names (like Bakbuk) may indicate they were descriptive nicknames given to foreign servants rather than formal genealogical names.
52: Bᵉnê baṣlût mᵉhîḏāʾ wᵉḥaršāʾ — Bazluth, Mehida, and Harsha. Bāṣlût may be related to a root meaning 'in the shadow of'; Mᵉhîḏāʾ may be Aramaic; Ḥaršāʾ = 'artificer/craftsman' (from ḥāraš) — a surname indicating a family of craftsmen who entered temple service. The craftsman families (cf. Solomon's servants in vv55-57) represent skilled workers who served the temple in non-priestly roles.
53: Bᵉnê ḇarqôs sîsᵉrāʾ wᵉṯāmaḥ — Barkos, Sisera, and Thamah. Bārqôs may relate to 'son of Kos' (Edomite deity) suggesting Edomite ancestry; Sîsᵉrāʾ is the name of the Canaanite general defeated by Deborah and Jael (Judg 4-5) — whether this family traced Canaanite ancestry to Sisera's clan or the name was coincidental is unknown. The inclusion of names associated with Israel's traditional enemies (Canaanites, Edomites) among the temple servants confirms the broad integration of conquered peoples into the cult service.
54: Bᵉnê nᵉṣîaḥ wᵉḥāṭîpāʾ — Neziah and Hatipha. Nᵉṣîaḥ = 'preeminent/conspicuous' (Hebrew); Ḥāṭîpāʾ = possibly 'seized/taken' (from ḥāṭap = to snatch away), which could describe the origin of this family as captives taken in war and dedicated to temple service. This verse ends the Nethinim list proper (vv43-54 = 12 Nethinim groups).
55: Bᵉnê ʿaḇḏê šᵉlōmōh — the descendants of Solomon's servants: a second category of temple servants, distinct from the Nethinim. Solomon's servants (ʿaḇḏê šᵉlōmōh) appear in 1 Kgs 9:20-21 — the remnant Canaanite peoples Solomon conscripted for forced labor (mas-ʿōleh), who were not enslaved but levied for permanent state service. Over generations these 'Solomon's servants' became a hereditary class associated with the temple. Sotai, Sophereth, and Peruda begin the list; Sôṭay may derive from Aramaic; Sōpereṯ = 'scribe-woman' (feminine participle of sāpar = to write/count) — perhaps the female ancestor of a scribal family who served Solomon.
56: Bᵉnê yaʿălāh darqôn wᵉgiddēl — Jaalah, Darkon, and Giddel. Yaʿălāh = 'ibex/mountain goat' (same root as yaʿēl, the hero Jael's name); Darqôn = possibly from dereq or foreign; Giddēl = 'greatly magnified' (appears also in the Nethinim list, v47 — the same name in both Nethinim and Solomon's servants suggests name repetition in these lists, not family identity).
57: Bᵉnê šᵉpaṭyāh ḥaṭṭîl pōḵereṯ haṣṣᵉḇāyîm wᵉʾāmî — Shephatiah, Hattil, Pochereth-hazzebaim, and Ami. Pōḵereṯ-haṣṣᵉḇāyîm = 'binder of the gazelles' — an evocative compound name suggesting an ancestor who hunted or managed gazelles for the royal court. The long compound name is unusual in the list and may preserve an occupational title that became hereditary. Ami (Neh 7:59 reads 'Amon') is a short form that could be a throne name or abbreviated theophoric.
58: Kol-hannᵉtînîm ûḇᵉnê ʿaḇḏê šᵉlōmōh šᵉlōš mēʾôt tišʿîm ûšnāyim — all the Nethinim and Solomon's servants: 392 total. Neh 7:60 = 392 (agreement). The combined total (392) represents the full sub-temple-servant community. In Ezra 8:20, 220 Nethinim return with Ezra on the second return, showing the community's continued presence and modest growth. The combined Nethinim/Solomon's servants (392) plus the cultic staff (priests ~4,289; Levites 74; singers 128-148; gatekeepers 139) total roughly 4,000-4,600 cultic personnel returning with the first wave — a substantial religious infrastructure for the restored community.
59: The category of those who could not prove their ancestry (wᵉʾēlleh hāʿōlîm mittᵉl-melaḥ tᵉl-ḥaršāʾ kᵉrûḇ ʾaddān wᵉʾimmēr) — five Babylonian sites where uncertain-ancestry returnees originated. Tel-melah ('mound of salt'), Tel-harsha ('mound of the craftsman'), Cherub, Addan, and Immer are Babylonian locations; their names suggest geographic/topographic sites in the Babylonian exile territory where Judean communities had settled. Lōʾ yāḵᵉlû lᵉhaggîḏ bêt-ʾăḇôtêhem wᵉzarʿām = 'they could not declare/prove their ancestral house and their seed (descent).' The inability to prove ancestry is a crucial legal problem in the restoration community, where genealogy determined land-rights, priestly service eligibility, and covenant-community membership.
60: Bᵉnê dᵉlāyāh bᵉnê ṭôḇiyyāh bᵉnê nᵉqôḏāʾ — Delaiah, Tobiah, and Nekoda, 652. Neh 7:62 = 642 (difference of 10). The name Tobiah ('YHWH is good') is the name of Nehemiah's primary antagonist (Neh 2:10, 19; 4:3, etc.) — a prominent post-exilic family. The Tobiah connection suggests the family of uncertain ancestry who returned may be ancestors of the later prominent Tobiad dynasty. These 652 returnees can participate in the community but their status is uncertain until genealogical verification is possible.
61: The priestly families of uncertain ancestry — Habaiah, Hakkoz, and Barzillai — present a more serious problem. Habballyāh (Habaiah), haqqôṣ (Hakkoz), and the descendants of Barzillai who 'took a wife from the daughters of Barzillai the Gileadite.' Barzillai the Gileadite was a supporter of David during Absalom's revolt (2 Sam 17:27-29; 19:31-39); his priestly descendant had taken a Gileadite wife and adopted the father-in-law's name, blurring the genealogical record. The Hakkoz family appears in 1 Chr 24:10 as the seventh priestly division — their descendants are deregistered for the restoration period but later rehabilitated: Neh 3:4 and 3:21 show Meremoth son of Uriah son of Hakkoz actively participating in wall-repair, suggesting the question was eventually resolved.
62: Wayᵉḇaqqᵉšû kᵉtāḇām hammityaḥᵉśîm wᵉlōʾ nimṣāʾû waygōʾălû min-hakkᵉhunnāh — 'they sought their names in the genealogical register (kᵉtāḇ hammityaḥᵉśîm) but could not find them, and they were excluded (waygōʾălû) from the priesthood as unclean (ṭāmēʾ).' The term kᵉtāḇ hammityaḥᵉśîm (the genealogical register = the book of those who registered themselves by genealogy) is the official priestly census document. Gāʾal (to exclude as impure/unfit) is used here in the cultic sense of declaring someone ritually unclean for sacred service — not permanent excommunication but suspension pending resolution. The same root gāʾal in the land-redemption law (Lev 25) means the kinsman-redeemer; here its secondary sense of 'to treat as unclean/defile' is in play.
63: Haṯṯîrᵉšāṯāʾ — the governor. Tiršātāʾ is a Persian loanword (from Old Persian tarsāta = 'the feared one/revered one') used as a title for the Persian-appointed governor. In Ezra it designates Sheshbazzar or Zerubbabel; in Neh 8:9 and 10:1 it designates Nehemiah. The use of a Persian administrative title for the community's leader reflects the post-exilic political reality: the governor operates within Persian imperial authority. The ruling that the excluded priestly families 'must not eat of the most sacred offerings until a priest stands with the Urim and Thummim' (ʿōmēd kōhēn lᵉʾûrîm wᵉlᵉtummîm) invokes the ancient oracular instrument for determining YHWH's judgment in disputed cases (Ex 28:30; Lev 8:8; Num 27:21). The Urim and Thummim had apparently ceased to function in the pre-exilic period (they are absent from later Second Temple descriptions); this ruling thus deferred the resolution to a future messianic priest — effectively suspending these families' priestly status indefinitely.
64: Kol-haqqāhāl kᵉʾeḥāḏ ʾarbaʿāh wᵉʾarbaʿîm ʾelep šᵉlōš mēʾôt wᵉšiššîm — the whole assembly together, 42,360. This is the grand total: 42,360 for the whole assembly (haqqāhāl kᵉʾeḥāḏ). The problem is famous: if you add up all the individual family totals in Ezra 2 they sum to approximately 29,818; Neh 7 sums to approximately 31,089. Neither sum reaches 42,360. The same discrepancy appears in both books. Proposed explanations: (1) the individual totals are selective and do not cover all groups; (2) the grand total was transmitted from a different source that counted additional groups; (3) rounding errors accumulated. The grand total 42,360 is probably the authoritative official Persian census figure, with the detailed list being an incomplete extract from it.
65: The servants (ʿăḇādîm wᵉšiprōtêhem): 7,337 male and female servants, plus 200 male and female singers (mᵉšōrᵉrîm ûmᵉšōrᵉrôt). Neh 7:67 = 245 singers (difference of 45 from Ezra's 200). These are secular singers (entertainment musicians for the households), distinct from the cultic singers (descendants of Asaph, v41). The presence of 7,337 servants in addition to 42,360 free returnees indicates the returnees included a substantial enslaved/servant population — likely those brought into service during the exile or purchased as laborers for the return journey and resettlement.
66: Horses: 736. Neh 7:68 = 736 (agreement). Horses were expensive animals in the ancient Near East, primarily used for military and prestige transport — not for agricultural work (which used donkeys). The presence of 736 horses among the returnees indicates significant wealth and military capacity within the returnee community. Persian period administrative texts confirm horses as status markers in the province of Yehud.
67: Mules: 245; camels: 435; donkeys: 6,720. Neh 7:69 = mules 245; camels 435; donkeys 6,720 (agreement across all three). The large number of donkeys (6,720) reflects the primary beast of burden for transport across the Palestinian landscape. Camels (435) were used for long-distance desert/caravan transport — relevant for the journey from Babylon. The detailed livestock count functions as a wealth inventory and as logistics data for the Persian administration: this many animals implies significant fodder requirements and organizational capacity for the march from Babylon to Jerusalem.
68: Ûmirāʾšê hāʾāḇôt bᵉḇôʾām ʾel-bêt YHWH ʾăšer bîrûšālaim hittᵉnadᵉḇû lᵉḇêt hāʾĕlōhîm — when the heads of the fathers' houses arrived at the house of YHWH in Jerusalem, they gave freewill offerings (hittᵉnadᵉḇû) toward rebuilding the house of God. The verb nāḏaḇ Hithpael = to volunteer oneself, to give spontaneously — the same root as nᵉdāḇôt (freewill offerings) in v5. The first act of the leaders upon arrival at the temple site is voluntary giving for reconstruction — before housing themselves, before administrative organization, before any civic activity. The priority of the cult over personal establishment is a theological statement.
69: The giving totals: 61,000 darics of gold (darkᵉmônîm zāhāḇ šiššîm wᵉʾeḥāḏ ʾelep), 5,000 minas of silver (kaspā ḥamēšet ʾălāpîm), and 100 priestly garments (mᵉʾāh kuttōnôt kōhănîm). Darkᵉmônîm is the Hebrew form of 'daric' — the Persian gold coin introduced by Darius I (522-486 BCE), appearing here and in 1 Chr 29:7 (anachronistically for David's era) and Neh 7:70-72. If the darics are genuinely from the Cyrus era (538 BCE), this represents the earliest attestation of the term; some scholars see the use of darics as evidence that Ezra was written or edited after Darius I's coinage. 61,000 darics is a very large amount (roughly 500-600 kg of gold). The inclusion of 100 priestly garments (kuttōnôt) alongside monetary gifts reflects the comprehensive provision for the cult: not just construction funds but the vestments required for priestly service once the temple is rebuilt.
70: Wayyēšᵉḇû hakkōhănîm wᵉhalᵉwiyyim ûmin-hāʿām wᵉhammᵉšōrᵉrîm wᵉhaššōʿărîm wᵉhannᵉtînîm bᵉʿārêhem wᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl bᵉʿārêhem — the priests, Levites, some of the people, the singers, the gatekeepers, and the temple servants settled in their towns, and all Israel in their towns. The settlement formula closes the census list: each social category is assigned to 'their towns' (bᵉʿārêhem) — the ancestral territorial holdings to which each family is entitled. The final phrase wᵉḵol-yiśrāʾēl bᵉʿārêhem (and all Israel in their towns) uses the comprehensive 'all Israel' formula, asserting that the returnee community constitutes the authentic continuation of the covenant people — not a remnant but 'all Israel' repatriated. The settlement narrative frames the census as completed: the return has landed, the people are distributed, and the community is positioned for the cult's restoration.
